title: new
description: Requesting memory at runtime with new — for a single value and, finally, for an array whose size isn't known until the program runs.
objectives: Allocate a single value at runtime with new
Allocate a dynamically-sized array with new[]
Finally solve the runtime-sized array problem from Dynamic Memory Allocation

sections.code:
int *ptr = new int; // allocates one int on the heap, uninitialized
*ptr = 25;

sections.text: new Type requests enough memory to hold one value of that type, at runtime, and returns a pointer to it. Recall Pointers: the * is then used to actually read or write the value stored there.

sections.code: int *ptr = new int(25); // allocated AND initialized to 25 in one step

sections.text: Parentheses after the type let new set an initial value immediately, avoiding a separate *ptr = 25; line.
3. new for an Array — Finally, Runtime Sizing

This solves exactly the problem raised in Dynamic Memory Allocation: int scores[n]; wasn't valid standard C++ because n wasn't known at compile time. new int[n] is completely valid — n is read from cin first, and the array is sized at that exact moment, while the program runs.
4. A Deterministic Example (Fixed Size, Still Dynamic)
This one uses a fixed 5 so its output is predictable to trace here — but new int[5] is still genuine dynamic allocation, indexed with [] exactly like an ordinary array from Arrays.

rows: A pointer to a single new int (int*)
A pointer to the first element of a new array of n ints (int*)

sections.text: Either way, new hands back a pointer — this is why a pointer variable is always needed on the left side to receive it, just like in Pointers.

sections.title: Ordering exactly the amount you need
sections.text: Instead of a fixed 5-slot tray baked into the kitchen, new int[n] is like ordering exactly n plates from the supplier the moment you find out how many guests are coming — decided at the last minute, sized precisely for the occasion.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting new returns a pointer, not a value — it must be assigned to a pointer variable, e.g. int *ptr = new int;.
Using delete instead of delete[] for an array allocated with new[] — this distinction is covered fully in the delete topic next, but they are not interchangeable.
Assuming a normal (non-new) array could have been sized with a runtime variable — that's exactly the limitation new[] exists to lift.
Losing track of a pointer returned by new without ever freeing it — covered next in delete, but worth flagging now: this memory doesn't clean itself up automatically.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does the new operator do?
questions.answer: It requests memory at runtime — for a single value or an array — and returns a pointer to the allocated memory.

questions.question: How does new solve the problem of array sizes needing to be known at compile time?
questions.answer: new int[n] allocates an array whose size, n, can be a variable computed or read at runtime, unlike an ordinary array declaration, which requires a fixed compile-time size.

questions.question: What type of value does new int[n] return?
questions.answer: A pointer to the first element of the newly allocated array (int*), just like new int returns a pointer to a single allocated int.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: new requests memory at runtime and returns a pointer to it — new int allocates a single value, while new int[n] allocates an array of n elements, where n can be a variable only known once the program is running. This is exactly what solves the limitation raised in Dynamic Memory Allocation, where an ordinary array's size has to be a fixed value known at compile time. Either form of new hands back a pointer, which must be stored in a pointer variable and, as covered next in delete, eventually released.

sections.title: Trick to Remember new
sections.text: new Type = order one item, made to order. new Type[n] = order n items, exactly how many you need, decided right now.
